summary: On Tuesday, the House voted 220 to 205 to extend government funding through Dec. 4, aiming to avert a shutdown before the November midterm elections.

House Appropriations Committee Chairman Tom Cole said the clean extension avoids disruptions as the funding process has stalled in the Senate with no annual appropriations bills cleared by the committee.

Democratic leaders opposed the measure, stating it "fails to block funding for Donald Trump 's violent mass deportation machine" and grants the administration excessive budget authority without reforms.
Senate Majority Leader John Thune said he would put the funding plan to a vote before the August recess, indicating he may use reconciliation to bypass Democratic opposition.

The legislative effort follows two government shutdowns earlier this Congress, with leaders hoping this short-term patch provides stability while avoiding partisan funding battles seen in previous months.
title: Source Icon

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the $37.5 billion estimate as proof the Iran war is “blowing a hole” in the budget, even a “quagmire” or “illegal war,” while right-leaning coverage treats the same figure as a “price tag” and a case for more funding, stressing financial pressure, readiness, and even Iran’s “weakest military point in decades.” The left uses sharper rebuke—“just admitted,” “blasted,” “suspiciously low”—whereas the right leans into disruption and control, with phrases like “War Secretary,” “arrested,” and “multiple times.” Both sides center cost and Senate scrutiny, but split over whether it signals waste or wartime necessity.
summary: On Tuesday, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth testified that the U.S. war against Iran has cost $37.5 billion, as he defended a $95 billion GOP budget package before the Senate Appropriations Committee.

The GOP budget plan represents a final priority before midterm elections, including $10 billion in aid for farmers struggling under President Donald Trump 's tariffs and $10 billion for voting law changes.
Pentagon reports show 17 service members have died and nearly 450 have been injured since the conflict began, figures Hegseth faced questions about as protesters interrupted his opening remarks.
Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer criticized Hegseth for marketing testosterone supplements instead of disclosing casualty numbers, while Senator Patty Murray warned of "another forever war."
House Speaker Mike Johnson continues pushing the budget resolution, asserting the administration is working toward resolution, though Trump has claimed over 40 times that a deal is near.
title: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth tells Congress that the latest estimate of the cost of the Iran war is $37.5 billion

bias: Left-Leaning coverage foregrounds cascading harm—“threatening,” “flooding,” “storm surge,” and evacuation or local impact concerns—casting Bertha as a broad regional risk, while right-leaning coverage more often pivots to preparedness and urgency, with phrases like “brace for impacts,” “serious danger,” and insurance or home-protection advice.
Center coverage is more process-driven, stressing tracking maps, parish-by-parish forecasts, and meteorological nuance like slow movement and dry air.
The main dividing line is whether the storm is framed as a public-safety problem, a practical readiness issue, or a forecast puzzle.
All sides still converge on heavy rain and possible flooding, reflecting shared concern but different ideological priorities.
summary: Tropical Storm Bertha formed Monday evening in the northeastern Gulf of Mexico with maximum sustained winds of 50 mph, centered about 125 miles south of Panama City, Florida, and moving slowly northwest at 2 mph.

A tropical blob of moisture that forecasters tracked for nearly a week organized into Tropical Depression Two on Sunday, July 19, around 11 a.m. in the northeastern Gulf, with NHC forecaster John Cangialosi confirming persistent convection justified its classification.

Rainfall totals of 2 to 4 inches are expected from western Florida to the middle Texas coast through Friday, with isolated amounts reaching 8 inches, while tropical-storm-force winds extend up to 80 miles from Bertha's center.

Storm surge watches extend from the Alabama-Florida border to the Mississippi River mouth, with potentially life-threatening inundation of up to 4 feet possible, and double red flags warning swimmers at Orange Beach, Alabama, of hazardous surf conditions.

Current forecasts place Bertha near or south of New Orleans by Wednesday before potentially reaching southeast Texas by Thursday, making it the second named storm of the 2026 Atlantic hurricane season after Tropical Storm Arthur.
title: Tropical Storm Bertha forms on track approaching US Gulf Coast

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the tariffs as a “dramatic” escalation that could trigger a “fresh trade war,” spotlighting inflation risk, “economic chaos,” and strained ties with a close ally; right-leaning coverage instead casts Trump as “slapping” or “holding Canada accountable” for “discriminatory” treatment, with some headlines stressing the “surprise” and his not warning Carney.

The key dividing lines are whether the move is portrayed as harmful overreach or justified retaliation, and whether the cited Canadian booze, dairy, and auto policies are treated as context or pretext.
Both sides agree the 50% tariffs escalate tensions, but they diverge on blame and legitimacy.
summary: On Monday, President Donald Trump imposed 50% tariffs on most Canadian goods, claiming Canada unfairly discriminated against American autos, alcohol, and dairy products.
Trump signed three proclamations invoking Section 338 of the 1930 Trade Act, a law permitting tariffs up to 50% without congressional approval when discrimination is alleged.
The 50% tariffs take effect in 30 days and exclude energy, potash, fish, and critical minerals but cover goods previously protected by the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement.
An administration official noted that Canada is one of the only nations besides China that retaliated against previous tariffs, stating the country must be held accountable.
Trump also asked aides to explore additional tariffs tied to Canadian wildfires affecting air quality, while negotiations from the expired 2020 trade pact may continue until 2036.
title: Trump imposing 50% tariffs on certain Canadian goods over alleged trade discrimination

bias: Left-Leaning coverage largely frames the episode as an anti-ICE protest-related confrontation, leaning on cautious wording like “accused” and “fireworks” while de-emphasizing the most weapon-heavy details; right-leaning coverage instead spotlights menace and motive, using sharper labels like “armed loon,” “maniac,” “terrorism,” and “anti-ICE attack” to cast it as a broader threat to immigration enforcement.
The biggest dividing lines are the suspect’s alleged ideology, the “incendiary device” versus “fireworks” framing, and the loaded “bad intentions”/“anti-American” cues.
Both sides agree on the custody, federal setting, and FBI probe, but they diverge sharply over whether the event signals activism or extremism.
summary: Federal authorities arrested 43-year-old Andrew Arrabaca, a former U.S. Army veteran, after he allegedly launched fireworks, poured flammable liquid, and ignited a fire outside 26 Federal Plaza in Lower Manhattan.
The Lower Manhattan building houses multiple federal agencies, including an immigration court, an FBI field office, and Immigration and Customs Enforcement operations.

Arrabaca came heavily armed with various non-firearm weapons, including an airsoft rifle, axes, a machete, knives, a hammer, and commercial-grade fireworks. FBI officials reported he fired five to seven pellets from an airsoft gun toward the entrance.

Law enforcement described Arrabaca as an anti-ICE activist, noting he carried anti-ICE materials and a cart displaying the slogan "ICE off our streets". Department of Homeland Security officials also noted he had a written manifesto in his bag.

Three minor injuries were reported during the incident, including a bystander grazed by fireworks and a security officer who sustained minor injuries while tackling the suspect to the ground before he was taken into custody.
title: Army Veteran in Custody After Fire Outside 26 Federal Plaza

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Biggs’s win as a warning sign for the GOP, stressing terms like “right-wing,” “fierce ally,” and his election-denial baggage, while right-leaning coverage turns the same result into momentum, leaning on “firebrand,” “high-stakes showdown,” and “frontrunner” to dramatize strength and competitiveness.
Center coverage is more procedural, emphasizing a Republican nomination and Arizona ’s “path back to power” in a battleground state.

The dividing lines are Trump ties, 2020 claims, and whether the race is a liability story or a power-play story; both sides agree Biggs won and will face Katie Hobbs, but they diverge on what that victory signifies.
summary: On Tuesday, U.S. Rep. Andy Biggs won the Republican nomination for Arizona governor, defeating fellow Rep. David Schweikert and advancing to face Democratic Gov. Katie Hobbs in November.
President Donald Trump's endorsement and support from Turning Point Action's grassroots turnout machine proved decisive, as Schweikert struggled to raise money and overcome Biggs' momentum.
Democrats plan to portray Biggs as an extremist, citing his tenure as chair of the House Freedom Caucus and his vote against certifying the 2020 election after the January 6, 2021, riot.
Biggs' nomination poses a strategic challenge for the GOP, as Arizona Republicans have lost recent statewide races after nominating Trump loyalists like Kari Lake four years ago.

Maricopa County officials reported two power outages at polling sites during the primary, underscoring scrutiny in a county managing two-thirds of the state's voters and an epicenter of election conspiracy theories.
title: Trump-backed Rep. Andy Biggs wins GOP primary for Arizona governor

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Rubio’s remarks as a guarded diplomatic posture, stressing that the U.S. is “willing to negotiate” and even “protect its interests and allies,” while right-leaning coverage turns the same exchange into a sharper conflict narrative, using terms like “false claims,” “not serious,” and “grip” to cast Iran as intransigent and the crisis as escalating.

The center adds the broadest context—“11 successive nights” of strikes and Trump’s “not finished” warning—while both sides share concern over disrupted oil routes and the Strait of Hormuz, revealing a common focus on energy chokepoints but different ideological readings of blame, leverage, and escalation.
summary: On Wednesday, U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio affirmed Washington remains open to negotiations with Iran, though he expressed skepticism regarding Tehran's intentions as the widening conflict disrupts critical Red Sea and Strait of Hormuz energy chokepoints.

The Iran-aligned Houthis announced a naval blockade on Saudi Arabia on Monday, threatening to attack oil tankers near the Bab el-Mandeb, known as the "Gate of Tears," prompting vessels to divert course.
With the U.S. military bombing targets across Iran for an 11th straight night on Tuesday, Brent crude prices climbed more than 2% to hover above $91 a barrel, reflecting market instability.

President Donald Trump renewed threats to attack Iranian nuclear facilities at Natanz, while Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth reported the war has cost the United States $37.5 billion, up from nearly $29 billion in May.

Despite the escalation, Iran requested that mediator Pakistan continue ceasefire efforts after receiving a proposal for a 10-day pause, though Rubio questioned whether Tehran remains serious about pursuing diplomatic resolution.
title: Rubio says US still willing to negotiate over Iran crisis

bias: The left frames the Manila meeting through the “Ayungin incident,” making it a flashpoint story, while the center turns it into cautious summit diplomacy with “possible” Trump-Xi talks and “alleged election meddling,” and the right broadens it into regional-security theater, stressing “maritime rights,” China ’s sovereignty claims, and “global tensions.” Tone diverges too: the center stays restrained, the left is conflict-oriented, and the right uses firmer, more charged phrasing like “stressed” and “flared.” The key dividing lines are whether the event is a bilateral crisis, a tentative diplomatic step, or a South China Sea power contest, even as all sides agree the talks sit inside wider U.S.-China strain.
summary: On Wednesday, U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio met Chinese Foreign Minister Wang in Manila during the China-ASEAN Foreign Ministers' Meeting to address escalating regional tensions.

Tensions have escalated following a reported clash at Ayungin Shoal, where the Philippine military claimed a sailor was injured by the China Coast Guard, prompting Washington to condemn the action as "dangerous and aggressive."

Rubio emphasized Washington's commitment to "freedom of navigation in Southeast Asia " in a Tuesday op-ed, while Wang accused "some forces within the Philippine military and police" of deliberately engaging in provocations.
Analysts suggest the talks serve as preparation for a potential summit between President Donald Trump and President Jinping, with Trump stating Jinping will visit the United States in September.

Complicating diplomatic efforts, Trump renewed accusations last week regarding Chinese election meddling, which Beijing denied as "pure fabrications," though Rubio noted it remains "incumbent on powerful and important countries" to maintain dialogue.
title: Rubio meets China's Wang as US, China prepare for possible Xi visit

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the Arizona primaries as ideology-and-influence battles, stressing “progressive,” “uphill,” and even “election meddling” around Israel/AIPAC, while casting Trump ties through charged words like “Trump-Endorsed,” “Celebrity,” and “Shocking Sex Scandal.” Right-leaning coverage, by contrast, turns the Democratic race into a culture-war spectacle, repeatedly using “communist” and celebrating GOP wins with “storms to victory” and “backed by Trump.” Center-leaning coverage stays more procedural, “fends off” and “hammered” by issue critiques like corporate donations and the Laken Riley vote.
Both sides agree on the winners, but diverge sharply on whether the story is about local contests or broader partisan identity.
summary: On Tuesday, July 21, 2026, incumbent Democratic Rep. Greg Stanton defeated progressive challenger Kai Newkirk in Arizona's 4th Congressional District primary, according to Decision Desk HQ projections.

Challenging the moderate incumbent, Newkirk prioritized Israel and the American Israel Public Affairs Committee as central campaign issues while aiming to make the race a referendum on money in politics and resistance to President Donald Trump.

While Newkirk faced a flurry of lost endorsements, Stanton secured over $300K in AIPAC contributions and significant July spending from Protect Progress and Think Big, pro-cryptocurrency and AI industry groups respectively.
Advancing to the general election, Stanton will face Republican Zuhdi Jasser, who unsuccessfully sought the GOP nomination in 2024 in the blue district this fall.

Progressive challengers across the country are attempting to unseat incumbents this cycle, reflecting broader party tensions; Newkirk was one of dozens nationwide, though many struggle to gain local momentum.
title: Greg Stanton wins Democratic nomination for U.S. House in Arizona's 4th Congressional District

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the firing as an abuse of power, stressing “illegal/unlawful,” “fired,” and “install its own picks,” while right-leaning pieces pivot toward institutional conflict and partisan suspicion, using labels like “rogue judges,” “Democrat Interim,” and “purportedly fired” to cast doubt on the judges and Rogoff’s standing.
The key dividing line is whether the story is a Trump overreach case or a presidential authority fight over a judge-appointed prosecutor.
Center coverage stays more procedural, flagging the “54 minutes” detail and the lawsuit’s “first of its kind” legal stakes.
Both sides agree on the abrupt appointment-and-firing sequence and the broader legal challenge.
summary: Former judge Roger Rogoff filed a lawsuit on Tuesday challenging his firing by President Donald Trump , who terminated him just 54 minutes after his July 15 swearing-in as U.S. Attorney for the Western District of Washington.

The Western District Court appointed Rogoff to fill a vacancy remaining since mid-2023, while Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche asserted the president's authority to remove him, claiming the court ignored consultation processes.

Rogoff's lawsuit argues his removal violates federal statutes and the Constitution's Appointments Clause, which allow district judges to appoint prosecutors when interim 120-day terms expire without Senate confirmation.

This legal standoff mirrors previous disputes in Northern Virginia, New Jersey, and New York where the Justice Department challenged court-appointed prosecutors, leaving prosecutorial authority legally jeopardized.

The case tests executive branch power against the judiciary's role in filling vacancies; if successful, the lawsuit would force the administration to respect judicial appointments and resolve the ongoing struggle over prosecutorial control.
title: Judges' pick for Seattle's federal prosecutor sues Trump after ouster

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the dispute as a democracy threat, using charged phrases like “dangerous for democracy” and “throws fit” to cast Kalshi’s pushback as reckless; right-leaning coverage turns the same issue into a law-and-order morality play, with lines like “don’t like our laws? Ignore them” and “gut the office” sharpening anti-establishment outrage.
Center coverage stays closer to the warning itself, stressing that a bet could “cost you your vote,” while noting prediction markets ’ growing popularity.

The dividing line is whether election wagering is treated as civic harm, voter misconduct, or a broader ideological test; all sides still converge on the commission’s warning and the Kalshi/Polymarket backlash.
summary: The Wisconsin Elections Commission warns voters that betting on election outcomes and then voting in that same election is illegal and can lead to prosecution and disqualification of their votes citing state statutes.

Prediction markets such as Kalshi and Polymarket allow peer-to-peer betting on election results but are currently unregulated at the federal level, with 23 states having laws that restrict such election betting.
Governor Tony Evers issued an executive order prohibiting state employees from using nonpublic information for personal gain through prediction markets.
Wisconsin statutes classify intentional voting while wagering on election outcomes as a felony, and the commission has emphasized legal consequences although it cannot monitor all betting activity.
title: Wisconsin Elections Commission Warns Voters Not to Bet on Election Outcomes

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as an urgent public- health failure, stressing a “deadly outbreak,” the fifth death, and that officials “still can’t find” the source, with a sharper blame edge; right-leaning coverage is briefer and more alarming in a different way, using terse phrases like “haven’t identified” and “more than six dozen” to underscore ongoing risk without the same accountability emphasis.

The center adds procedural detail—cooling towers, disinfection, and a possible lawsuit—while the main dividing line is whether the story is cast as government failure, an unfolding investigation, or a severe but contained crisis.
All sides agree the source remains unknown, tying the coverage to broader tensions over institutional competence and public safety.
summary: On Tuesday, health officials reported a fifth death in New York City's Legionnaires' disease outbreak, which has sickened at least 72 people over the past month.
Following the 2025 Harlem outbreak, the Health Department hired 23 additional Legionella specialists and conducted nearly 1,600 cooling tower inspections, a 35% increase from last year.

After detecting Legionella in 34 systems across 33 locations, the Health Department cleaned and disinfected 82 cooling towers at 80 buildings, including The Guggenheim Museum in Manhattan's Upper East Side.
Health Commissioner Alister F. Martin reported an encouraging sign with no new cases in the past 10 days, as eight patients remain hospitalized and 56 have been discharged.

Attorney Jory Lange, representing 50 victims of the 2025 Harlem cluster, warned that existing regulations are failing to prevent recurrent outbreaks, stating, "There's no excuse for this to keep happening.
title: 5th person dies in Legionnaires’ disease outbreak in New York City

summary: On Wednesday, Australian Foreign Minister Penny Wong raised concerns about Beijing's nuclear-capable missile test with Foreign Minister Wang Yi, calling the launch "destabilising" during their meeting in Manila.

China conducted the test earlier this month, firing a ballistic missile with a dummy warhead into the Pacific Ocean that landed near the exclusive economic zones of Tuvalu and Kiribati, 1,000km northeast of the Solomon Islands.

Wong also criticized Beijing's "dangerous conduct" following a clash on Monday at the Second Thomas Shoal, where a Philippine sailor was allegedly struck with a baton, reiterating Australia's "real concerns about behaviour that is destabilising and risky."
On Tuesday, Wong announced a donation of 413 million pesos in drone technology and operator training to the Philippine Coast Guard during a visit to a coast guard vessel.

The 18-member Pacific Islands Forum failed to draft a united statement against the test during their meeting in Suva, Fiji, on Wednesday, as diverse ties with Beijing complicate regional consensus efforts.
title: Australia Protests China's 'Destabilising' Missile Test

summary: The newly appointed British prime minister inherits the enduring challenge of revitalizing a sluggish national economy that has experienced decades of weak growth.
Policymakers intend to implement a strengthened industrial strategy focused on safeguarding sovereign manufacturing and production capabilities in critical domestic sectors.
The government plans to pursue a massive devolution of power and financial authority to regional leaders in an effort to boost productivity across underperforming areas.
Elevated energy expenses and persistent cost-of-living pressures continue to constrain household budgets and hinder broader economic momentum.
Business leaders emphasize that maintaining a predictable and competitive tax environment is essential for unlocking private investment and ensuring long-term prosperity.
title: New UK Prime Minister Faces Challenge to Revive Slow-Growing Economy

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the canceled ceremony as a diplomatic rebuke to Trump’s “tariff threats,” stressing words like “inappropriate” and “trade threats” to portray the decision as a principled response to U.S. pressure; right-leaning coverage compresses the story into a harder-edged trade clash, using phrases like “called off” and foregrounding Trump’s promised “50% tariffs,” while de-emphasizing the official rationale and the Canada -only celebration.
The center leans more procedural, noting the bridge still opens and the event was simply cancelled.
Both sides agree the joint celebration is off, but they diverge sharply on whether the key story is political provocation, economic leverage, or routine administrative fallout.
summary: On Tuesday, Canada's federal government cancelled the planned cross-border opening ceremony for the Gordie Howe International Bridge, with Infrastructure Minister Gregor Robertson's office calling the joint event "inappropriate" following U.S. tariff threats.

President Donald Trump announced Monday he would impose 50 per cent tariffs on Canadian products within 30 days, forcing Canada to abandon the binational celebration originally planned for the infrastructure link.
A Canada-only celebration will proceed on Friday, and the Gordie Howe International Bridge remains set to open next Monday as originally scheduled.
The bridge's opening was delayed previously after the Trump administration sought to renegotiate the deal following pushback from the Moroun family, owners of the competing Ambassador Bridge.

Despite these diplomatic tensions, the vital trade link connecting Windsor, Ontario and Detroit remains on schedule to open next Monday, standing as a major infrastructure development between the United States and Canada.
title: No cross-border ceremony for Gordie Howe bridge in light of U.S. trade threats: minister

bias: Left-Leaning coverage foregrounds a concrete classroom injury: a faculty member “self-censored,” was told not to use “disparity,” and taught an “underserved population” under “altered” directives, framing the dispute as academic overreach and harm to education .

Right-Leaning coverage de-emphasizes that classroom drama and instead spotlights the lawsuit’s broader claims about limits on teaching sex, race, gender identity, and sexual orientation, using cooler legal language.
The central dividing line is whether this is portrayed as local censorship or a wider policy fight.
Both sides agree a lawsuit was filed; the split reflects deeper battles over campus power and culture-war boundaries.
summary: On Friday, the American Association of University Professors requested an injunction in U.S. district court for the Western District of Texas El Paso Division to block Texas Tech University System curriculum restrictions on race, gender, and sexual orientation instruction.

Chancellor Brandon Creighton issued directives in December 2025 and April 2026 under Senate Bill 37, which mandates administrators at the system's five institutions review course materials for prohibited subjects including race and gender identity.

By early May 2026, campus officials had evaluated about 8,500 courses, with the board's Academic, Clinical and Student Affairs Committee assessing approximately 500 and modifying dozens of previously approved classes.

An anonymous Texas Tech Health instructor, identified as 'Member No.,' self-censored coursework after officials instructed them to avoid the word 'disparity,' restricting training on LGBTQIA patient care.

Senior counsel Antonio Ingram II of the Legal Defense Fund expects the case to reach federal court in El Paso this summer, arguing the directives violate the First Amendment based on a recent 11th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals ruling.
title: Lawsuit challenges Texas Tech directives that altered teaching at El Paso medical school

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as a pragmatic correction, stressing that Mamdani “acknowledges” or “doesn’t have authority,” while right-leaning outlets turn it into a saga of reversal, using sharper language like “goes back,” “admits,” “walks back,” and “BREAKING” to cast him as exposed or performative.

The biggest split is the legal-versus-moral frame: the center notes the ICC warrant and “legal authority,” the right amplifies “war criminal” and “not welcome,” and the left de-emphasized the broader Israel /ICC context.
Both sides agree he shifted from a campaign pledge, but they diverge on whether that signals responsible clarification or political embarrassment.
summary: On Tuesday, New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani released a video declaring Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu a "war criminal" and urging the federal government to execute an International Criminal Court arrest warrant.
After his administration "has reviewed every avenue available under applicable law," Mamdani acknowledged New York City lacks the independent legal authority to enforce the warrant himself.
The International Criminal Court warrant alleges Netanyahu is "allegedly responsible for the war crimes of starvation as a method of warfare" spanning October 8, 2023, through May 20, 2024.
President Donald Trump stated Monday via Truth Social that Netanyahu "will not be arrested, in any way, shape or form, while in the United States," directly countering Mamdani's federal call.
United Nations Danny Danon responded on X, "Enough with the attacks on Jewish organizations," accusing the mayor of serving Hamas propaganda and undermining New York service.
title: Mamdani acknowledges he doesn’t have authority to arrest Netanyahu

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the deal as a risky Trump move that “may allow” uranium enrichment, stressing caution, oversight, and the “big win for the Kingdom” as a sign of Saudi advantage and possible U.S. leverage through firms and “expanded oversight.” Right-leaning coverage instead foregrounds the agreement as “landmark” and development-oriented, emphasizing strategic opportunity and the prospect of a joint study or enrichment facility.
Center-Leaning coverage sits between them, adding “missing safeguards” and congressional timing.
All sides agree Trump approved a 30-year civilian nuclear pact and that enrichment is the key fault line.
summary: President Donald Trump formally approved a civilian nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia that could permit uranium enrichment on Saudi soil, according to The Wall Street Journal.
The agreement, expected to be announced as early as Wednesday, establishes a 30-year partnership positioning American firms to lead development of Saudi Arabia's nuclear infrastructure.
Following a joint U.S.-Saudi study, the deal could facilitate construction of a uranium enrichment facility within Saudi Arabia, raising proliferation concerns in the region.
American firms are positioned to capture significant profits from the infrastructure work, as the deal excludes other foreign competitors from the primary construction role.
Concerns regarding nuclear proliferation in the Middle East persist, with the Journal noting the deal likely raises questions about technology spread across the region.
title: Trump Approves Landmark Nuclear Deal With Saudi Arabia in Big Win for Kingdom: Report

summary: In Thakur v. Trump court filings last week, federal agencies admitted using keywords like "health equity" and "structural racism" to screen and terminate more than 1,000 research grants at the University of California.

Lawyers for the researchers contend this admission confirms the illegal cancellation of nearly $2 billion in funding, with agencies targeting projects in states supporting Kamala Harris while leaving grants in "red" states untouched.

The National Institutes of Health alone suspended or cancelled nearly 700 grants at UCLA, while The Department of Transportation terminated six projects worth about $42 million, forcing researchers to lay off more than 40 graduate and undergraduate assistants.

Arguing agencies bypassed required assessments, plaintiffs asked Judge Rita F. Lin to issue a final ruling ordering grant restoration, while The Environmental Protection Agency moves toward settlement to avoid further litigation.

The health agency suggested Elon Musk's short-lived DOGE office may have used AI to target grants for termination, with a court hearing scheduled for Oct. 20 to determine whether funding will be permanently restored.
title: White House admits it used keywords to kill billions worth of California research grants

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the dispute as a voter-access and fairness fight, stressing that “barring” revotes could “disenfranchise” thousands or leave ballots “worthless,” while right-leaning coverage treats it more as a party-driven procedural reversal amid “upheaval,” spotlighting that Democrats “consulted” campaigns before asking the commission to change “guidance.” The emotional registers diverge: left language leans on “chaotic” and rights-based phrasing, while right outlets use more skeptical, process-focused terms like “reversal” and “recast.” The key dividing lines are the returned absentee ballots , the “rule change,” and candidate dropouts, though both sides agree the commission’s guidance is the central flashpoint in a broader debate over election administration and participation.
summary: Wisconsin Democrats want absentee voters who cast ballots for Sara Rodriguez to be allowed to submit new primary ballots after she ended her 2024 governor campaign and David Crowley rejoined with Governor Tony Evers' endorsement.
More than 80,000 absentee ballots have been returned, but current Wisconsin Elections Commission guidance prohibits voters who submitted ballots for Rodriguez from receiving new ballots.
The Democratic Party argues this guidance must be reversed, citing a 2006 U.S. Supreme Court ruling and prior commission rules to ensure full voter participation in the primary.
This dispute is expected to lead to a lawsuit with the August 11 primary about three weeks away, amid Democrats' hopes to retain the governorship and gain full control of state government.
title: Wisconsin Democrats Want to Allow Absentee Voters to Cast a New Primary Ballot

bias: Center-Leaning coverage leans into the drama of the incident, using sharper terms like “attacked” and “mauling” to foreground the woman’s injury and recovery, while right-leaning coverage partly softens the event as a “bear encounter” and shifts attention to public-safety logistics, including ADF&G, police, firefighters, and drone tracking.
That split creates a key dividing line: one side heightens victim-centered urgency, the other highlights wildlife response and management.
Both agree on the same core facts—south Anchorage, three dogs, non-life-threatening injuries—but they frame the episode through different lenses of danger, authority, and community risk.
summary: On Saturday, July 18, 2026, a hiker was hospitalized after a brown bear mauled her on a trail in Bear Valley, a neighborhood in South Anchorage, Alaska , according to police.
The Alaska Department described the site as 'a social trail' where dense alder thickets and high winds likely obscured the bear, increasing odds of a surprise encounter.
Anchorage Police Department and Firefighters deployed a drone to track the bear and ensure safety, then carried the injured woman about a half-mile down the trail to a hospital.
Initial reports that the hiker's dog was killed were 'fortunately proven untrue,' the Game Department confirmed, after a different hiking group later found the pet uninjured.
Alaska is sometimes referred to as 'bear country,' yet bear attacks on humans remain generally rare, though officials continue monitoring recent activity in the region.
title: Woman attacked by brown bear while hiking Alaskan trail

bias: Left-Leaning coverage stays terse, centering the Pentagon ’s identification and the soldier’s presumed death, while right-leaning pieces turn it into a sharper conflict story, using phrases like “enemy attack,” “widening regional escalation,” and highlighting that U.S. forces were launching “strikes” on Iran.
Center coverage sits between them, adding unit details and human-interest touches that personalize the loss.
The key dividing line is whether the event is framed mainly as a casualty update or as part of a broader war dynamic.
Both sides agree on the identification, hometown ties, and rising toll, but they diverge on how much geopolitical urgency to attach.
summary: On Tuesday, the Defense Department identified Sgt. Angel S. Rampersad, 28, of Ozone Park, New York, as the third U.S. service member killed during an Iranian attack on Muwaffaq Salti Air Base in Jordan.

Military officials updated Rampersad's status from missing to 'believed to be deceased' after unidentified remains were discovered on Sunday, though an examination process remains ongoing to verify identity.

The Pentagon previously identified Pvt. Isabella Gonzales, 19, and 1st Tyler James Feehan, 25, as the first two soldiers killed in the July 17 assault. Rampersad served with the 1st Battalion, 57th Air Defense Artillery Regiment based in Ansbach, Germany.

These latest casualties bring the total American death toll in the war with Iran to 18. Defense Department records indicate more than 400 U.S. troops have been wounded in the campaign since February 28.
President Donald Trump vowed to strike Iran's Pickaxe Mountain facility soon, as Central Command announced a new round of strikes for an 11th consecutive night, effectively escalating the conflict.
title: US military identifies 3rd service member believed to have been killed in Iranian attack

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the mission as a constructive, almost restorative effort, using phrases like “give extra life” and the colorful “out-of-gas” to stress usefulness and renewal; right-leaning coverage stays more stripped-down, offering only the headline-level mission summary and de-emphasized elaboration.

The center version stands apart by adding vivid operational detail—“blasted off,” “private salvage operation,” and “life-extending jetpacks”—which sharpens the story’s technical rescue angle without politicizing it.

The main dividing line is emphasis: left highlights benefit, right minimizes narrative color, and all three largely agree this is a private satellite-lifespan extension mission tied to fuel limits and orbital maintenance.
summary: A two-armed space robot was launched on a private mission to attach life-extending jetpacks to old, fuel-depleted satellites orbiting thousands of miles above Earth.
This mission is the second of its kind launched this month, aiming to extend the operational life of aging satellites and save costs.
Northrop Grumman's robotic mission vehicle, launched by SpaceX, will spend a year reaching orbit 22,300 miles above Earth to perform the extensions.
Northrop Grumman is collaborating with the U.S. Naval Research Laboratory and the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency on this satellite-saving effort.
title: Private mission launches to extend life of out-of-gas communication satellites

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the death as a grim outcome of an ICE chase, using language like “died running from ICE” and “tourist with a ticket home” to stress lawful presence, sympathy, and enforcement overreach; it also adds biographical detail, like his job and hometown, to humanize him.

Right-Leaning coverage is far more stripped down, emphasizing the “ICE encounter,” “vacation,” and tourist visa without the same emotional layering, often turning the incident into a concise enforcement story.
The key dividing lines are whether ICE is portrayed as the central threat and whether the man’s travel status signals innocence.
Both sides still converge on the basic fatal sequence and tourist-visa status.
summary: On July 14, Juan Jairo Coronilla Duran, a 28-year-old Mexican tourist, died after fleeing an Immigration and Customs Enforcement stop near State Road 16 in St. Augustine, Florida, when struck by a semi-truck.
Coronilla Duran arrived in the United States three weeks ago on a valid tourist visa, vacationing with his wife, Jessica Alamilla, and was scheduled to return to Mexico this past Saturday.
According to witness Jose Rivera, unmarked SUVs stopped the vehicle before Coronilla Duran fled on foot, slipping and falling into the path of an oncoming semi-truck on the highway median.

Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum launched an investigation into the death, stating her government intends to pursue criminal charges regarding this incident and at least 18 other Mexican nationals killed during arrests.

Defending ICE operations Monday, Tom Homan, President Trump's 'border czar,' blamed casualties on 'hateful rhetoric' against the agency; this death marks the latest of 11 fatal shootings by federal immigration agents since the administration returned to office.
title: Mexican Man Killed After ICE Encounter in Florida Was on Vacation, Officials Say
Who Is Hamas’s New Leader, Khalil Al-Hayya?

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames AIPAC as “dropping” Democrats and even stripping “plaudits,” centering donor punishment and intra-party signaling; right-leaning coverage casts the same move as “freezes out” or retaliation against Democrats who “voted against aid to Israel,” treating the vote as a rebuke of an ally.
Centered coverage stays more procedural, noting the fundraising freeze and the $3.3 billion aid cut.
The sharpest dividing line is whether the vote is a policy disagreement or an anti-Israel act.
Both sides agree AIPAC selectively removed donation options, but the ideological split turns on loyalty, pressure, and the meaning of support for Israel.
summary: On Friday, the American Israel Public Affairs Committee suspended fundraising for more than two dozen House Democrats who backed an amendment to slash $3.3 billion in military aid to Israel, removing donation links from its online portal.
Rep. Thomas Massie introduced the measure to the fiscal 2027 State Department funding bill, which was defeated Wednesday by a 104-314-10 vote despite support from more than 100 Democrats.

Affected members include House Minority Whip Katherine Clark and former Speaker Nancy Pelosi, who received roughly $1.4 million and more than $19,000 respectively in AIPAC-related contributions according to Federal Election Commission records.

Defending his vote on Thursday, Rep. Jake Auchincloss stated Congress "must act where the executives have failed," as the suspension reflects AIPAC's consistent strategy of linking campaign support to voting records, previously applied to 15 Republican lawmakers in 2024.

Legislative records show 104 Democrats voted to cut aid this week, reflecting a broader realignment within the Democratic Party regarding unconditional support for Israel amid the humanitarian crisis in Gaza.
title: AIPAC halts fundraising for endorsed House Democrats who opposed Israel aid

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Abu Safiya as a humanitarian victim of deliberate, possibly lethal mistreatment, using charged terms like “imminent danger,” “deliberate,” and “scandalous,” and often broadening the story into attacks on doctors, hospitals, and calls for sanctions.
Right-Leaning coverage pivots toward security suspicion, with phrases like “Hamas colonel” and hints of “systematic targeting” or militant imagery, treating the case through a counterterror lens.
Center coverage stays more restrained, stressing “arbitrary” detention and UN/legal findings.
All sides agree he is detained and that UN bodies want his release, but ideology divides the interpretation of why.
summary: On Wednesday, a United Nations inquiry labeled the 18-month detention of Dr. Hussam Abu Safiya, director of Gaza's Kamal Adwan Hospital, arbitrary and urged his immediate release, citing violations of international human rights instruments.

Israeli forces seized Abu Safiya in December 2024, alleging the pediatrician was a Hamas member, though rights groups note authorities have produced no public evidence supporting these combatant claims during his detention without charge.

Lawyer Nasser Odeh reported after a July 2 prison visit that the doctor is nearly unrecognizable, suffering severe injuries to his 'head, eyes, ears, and neck' while struggling to breathe in the Rakefet interrogation facility.
Multiple UK Members of Parliament, including Jeremy Corbyn, former Labour leader, and Nadia Whittome, Labour MP, have urged the government to use diplomatic leverage to secure Abu Safiya's release.

The UN inquiry warned this case 'may indicate a widespread or systematic practice' of arbitrary detention, while UN Special Rapporteur Francesca Albanese criticized the BBC for presenting unproven Israeli military claims regarding the doctor.
title: UN inquiry urges release of Gaza doctor held by Israel since December 2024

bias: Left-Leaning coverage pairs Rubio’s warning with diplomacy, stressing that Washington stays open to talks while noting Iran’s past failure to honor commitments and the threat to the world economy and freedom of navigation.

Right-Leaning coverage is sharper and more hawkish, using phrases like “dangerous precedent,” “control,” and “cannot be accepted” to frame Iranian influence as an outright security threat, then widening the lens to China and South China Sea tensions.
Center coverage sits between them, echoing the warning in a more restrained way.
Both sides agree the Strait matters globally, but they diverge on whether the story is mainly about negotiation or deterrence.
summary: On Wednesday, U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio warned ASEAN foreign ministers in Manila that Iranian control of the Strait of Hormuz would set a dangerous precedent threatening the global economy and international commerce.
Rubio reaffirmed Washington's commitment to safeguarding commercial shipping and protecting global economic stability against ongoing security threats.
"This is an international waterway," Rubio said, emphasizing that the fundamental principle of freedom of navigation in the Middle East is currently threatened and cannot be allowed to fail.

ASEAN foreign ministers expressed "serious concern" over the conflict's impact on regional trade and energy markets, calling on all parties to "exercise utmost self-restraint" and avoid further aggravating the situation.

Delivering $2.5 billion in strategic investments to Southeast Asia, the United States reinforced its commitment to the region, signaling that protecting freedom of navigation remains central to U.S. national interests.
title: Iran’s Demands over the Strait of Hormuz Would Set a Dangerous Precedent, Rubio Tells ASEAN Leaders

bias: Left-Leaning coverage is de-emphasized here, while center and right diverge sharply: the center frames the Manila meeting as a straightforward diplomatic exchange, using restrained phrases like “hold talks,” “reaffirms,” and “free Indo-Pacific,” whereas the right turns it into a harder-edged geopolitical story, stressing “strategic ties,” “trade agreement,” and the more charged “pharma tariff threat.” That right-leaning emphasis adds tension around tariffs, defence, and Quad diplomacy , portraying the talks as leverage-heavy rather than merely procedural.

Both sides still converge on the India-US partnership and shared issues like trade, energy, and security, reflecting a broader split between cooperative regional order and economic-nationalist rivalry.
summary: On Wednesday, External Affairs Minister S. Jaishankar met U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio in Manila to discuss the India-US Comprehensive Global Strategic Partnership, covering trade, energy, defence, critical minerals, and artificial intelligence.

Jaishankar is in Manila for a two-day visit to participate in Foreign Minister-level meetings under the ASEAN framework, reflecting India's commitment to the Act East Policy and strengthening the ASEAN-India Comprehensive Strategic Partnership.

The External Affairs Minister also held talks with Philippines Foreign Secretary Ma Theresa P. Lazaro to expand the India-Philippines Strategic Partnership, and met Australian FM Senator Penny Wong to follow up on recent outcomes.

Reconvening on the sidelines of the ASEAN-related meetings, the Quad Foreign Ministers' Meeting reaffirmed the group's commitment to supporting ASEAN centrality and promoting a free and open Indo-Pacific, Secretary of State Marco Rubio said.

Rubio posted on X that "the Quad remains a priority, and we will meet again later this year," as the United States, India, Australia, and Japan share a vision of an Indo-Pacific grounded in sovereignty.
title: Indina FM Jaishankar, Marco Rubio Hold Talks in Manila on Trade, Energy

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Kolodin’s win as a warning sign, spotlighting that he “tried to overturn” election results and invoking “Trump mantle,” “election denialism,” and even “voting rights advocates” to cast the race as part of a larger threat to democratic norms.
Right-Leaning coverage stays spare and procedural, emphasizing the “Republican nomination” and the simple fact of victory, with the controversy largely de-emphasized.
The dividing line is Kolodin’s 2020 election challenge: one side treats it as the story, the other as background.
Both agree he won and will face Adrian Fontes, but they diverge sharply on whether that win signals routine politics or a broader election fight.
summary: On Tuesday, Arizona state representative Alex Kolodin won the Republican nomination for secretary of state, defeating Gina Swoboda and setting up a November general election against incumbent Democrat Adrian Fontes.

Kolodin built his campaign on skepticism toward election administration, drawing on his history as a lawyer who challenged 2020 results and represented Republican 'fake electors' seeking to overturn Arizona's certified outcome.
Polls showed Kolodin with a wide 44-20 lead over Swoboda, whom he accused of "carrying water" for Fontes, underscoring the GOP's shift toward candidates emphasizing past 2020 litigation efforts.

Maricopa County Recorder Stephen Richer warned that Kolodin's rhetoric could complicate state-county coordination, noting the secretary's role as chief election spokesperson could "damage confidence" in the democratic process.

The November contest pits Kolodin against Fontes, who defeated a prominent election denier in 2022 by more than 4 percent. The secretary of state office certifies election results and manages the state library and archives.
title: Arizona Republican Alexander Kolodin Wins GOP Nomination for Secretary of State

bias: Left and center coverage frame Trump’s Michigan trip as a routine campaign move tied to the primary, stressing timing, early voting, and the “battleground/swing state” context; the right adds a broader GOP strategy lens, saying Republicans want to “strengthen their position” for the 2026 midterms and highlighting Mike Rogers running unopposed.
The rhetorical split is subtle but real: the right’s “campaign” and “Heads To” style feels more momentum-driven, while the others stay more restrained.
The key dividing line is whether the visit is just a stop in an election calendar or part of a larger partisan push.
All three still agree Michigan is pivotal and politically competitive.
summary: President Donald Trump will hold a rally in Oakland County, Michigan, next week as early in-person voting begins ahead of the Aug. 4 primary election.
The Republican gubernatorial contest has intensified between Trump-backed U.S. Rep. John James and businessman Perry Johnson, who has spent millions on advertising attacking his rival.

Democrats face a competitive Senate primary between Rep. Haley Stevens and Abdul El-Sayed, while Michigan Republicans contend with surging gas prices, an unpopular war in Iran, and tariffs affecting the auto industry.
Oakland County, once a premier political bellwether for Michigan, has seen Democrats steadily expand their margins since 2016, making it a critical battleground for both parties.

With Republicans defending slim majorities in the House and Senate, the Aug. 4 primary serves as a pivotal test for candidate viability in a state both parties view as central to 2026 midterm elections.
title: President Donald Trump to travel to Michigan ahead of swing state’s primary election

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as a rights-versus-enforcement clash, spotlighting the judge as someone who “blocks” or “bars” deportation and casting the detainee as a “witness” and a “Mexican homebuilder,” with lawyers’ push for release emphasized as a broader civil-liberties fight.
The center leans procedural, stressing the “temporary” order and “continued ICE detention” while de-emphasizing the more activist tone.

Both sides share the grim “fatal shooting” and ICE traffic-stop context, but the dividing line is whether the case signals government overreach or simply a narrow legal ruling amid a broader immigration -enforcement dispute.
summary: On Monday, U.S. District Judge Keith Ellison ordered the Department of Homeland Security to halt the deportation of Jose Trinidad Rojas Pliego, a witness to the fatal July 7 shooting of Lorenzo Araujo Salgado in Houston.

The July 7 shooting involving an Immigration and Customs Enforcement officer prompted passengers Daniel Tirado Pantoja and Victor Hugo Salgado Araujo to join Rojas Pliego in filing petitions seeking release from detention.
Attorneys for the witnesses are pursuing U visas while disputing FBI claims of illicit substances, asserting the bags contained salt mixtures used for hydration during manual labor.
Under the directive, the Department of Homeland Security cannot transfer Rojas Pliego out of the Southern District of Texas while his petition is pending; the agency confirmed it will comply.
Mexico's government plans to file complaints regarding 17 deaths related to U.S. immigration enforcement, while protests continue to demand an independent investigation into the fatal Houston event.
title: Judge bars deportation of witness in fatal ICE shooting of Mexican homebuilder

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Fields as a lawmaker pushed aside by redistricting, stressing “no viable path,” “gutting,” and the loss of a majority-Black seat, while right-leaning coverage casts the same move as “jumping ship,” an “exit strategy,” and proof Republicans gain from a Court-linked map shift; center-leaning pieces stay more procedural, noting the seat was “eliminated” or “redrawn.” The sharpest divide is whether the story is about voting-rights harm and representation or Democratic retreat and GOP advantage.
Both sides agree on his switch to state Senate, but they interpret the stakes through opposing lenses on race, power, and party strength.
summary: On Tuesday, Louisiana Democrat Cleo Fields announced he will not seek reelection to Congress and will instead run for his former seat in the Louisiana Senate's 14th District.
Fields' decision follows a court-ordered redrawing of congressional boundaries that dismantled his majority-Black 6th District, now favoring Republicans in five of the state's six House districts.

The National Republican Congressional Committee announced Monday it is targeting the 6th District to flip the seat, with NRCC spokesman Reilly Richardson stating the district will elect a "commonsense conservative."
Fields seeks to fill the unexpired term of the late state Sen. Larry Selders, who died suddenly on July 7 following a heart attack.
The former congressman has scheduled a news conference for 10 a.m. Friday, July 24, to discuss his decision, saying his calling to serve is "better put to use back home.
title: Louisiana Democrat won’t run for reelection after redistricting

bias: Left-Leaning coverage centers the victims as a Honduran family caught in a quiet fishing outing, stressing the human loss and using softer phrases like “leisurely fishing” and even emotive weather language, while right-leaning coverage pivots toward “tragedy,” “rescue,” and “hidden river dangers,” recasting the event as a perilous rescue attempt with broader cautionary implications.
A key dividing line is whether the drownings happened during helping a swimmer, and the right also amplifies the children left “without any living relatives in US,” adding sharper emotional stakes.
Center coverage stays more restrained, mainly adding recovery-timeline details.
All sides still converge on the deaths, Honduran nationality, and family ties.
summary: Five adults drowned after being swept into the Scioto River at Shaughnessy Reservoir near Powell while attempting to assist a struggling swimmer, according to Delaware County Sheriff Jeffrey Balzer.
Heather Byers of the Ohio Department of Natural Resources warned that rivers can appear deceptively calm on the surface, even as water depths shift abruptly from 2 to 3 feet to 4 or 5 feet.

A passing driver who called 911 reported a child wrapped in a towel fleeing the riverbank screaming for his mother; authorities recovered the two women's bodies Sunday and the three men's bodies Monday.

Honduran authorities confirmed Tuesday that four of the five adults were Honduran citizens, including Jose Mario Pineda Dias and Marina Suyapa Regalado; Flabia Zamora, director of consular affairs at the Honduran Foreign Ministry, said repatriation could take at least a month.

The two surviving children, ages 8 and 10, remain in Ohio state care while officials arrange reunification with relatives in Honduras; relative Brenda Raya launched a GoFundMe to cover funeral, transportation, and childcare costs.
title: 2 married couples from Honduras were among 5 adults who drowned in an Ohio river

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Cory Mills as a Trump-endorsed Republican who was “blindsided” by a DOJ probe, stressing surprise and political accountability; right-leaning coverage turns the same story into a scandal narrative, using terms like “BREAKING,” “AGAIN,” and “scandal-plagued” to suggest repeated wrongdoing and a broader pattern of misconduct.

The sharpest dividing lines are the left’s focus on his unexpected discovery of the investigation and the right’s expansion into domestic violence and “stolen valor” allegations, while center coverage stands apart by emphasizing procedural details like subpoenas, documents, and witnesses.
All sides agree on the DOJ and ethics scrutiny, but differ on whether it signals surprise, procedure, or collapse.
summary: The Department of Justice opened a criminal investigation into Rep. Cory Mills, R-Fla., according to multiple sources, with investigators from the Middle District of Florida examining his finances and alleged overseas business dealings.

Mills already faces a House Ethics Committee investigation launched in November 2025 examining campaign finance violations, misuse of congressional resources, and sexual misconduct allegations, with the committee authorizing more than 20 subpoenas and contacting dozens of witnesses as of May.

At least one person was contacted by the FBI last year regarding Mills' finances, allegations of misconduct and individuals in his orbit, while Mills was first elected to Florida's 7th Congressional District in 2022 and faces an August primary.

Mills' office declined immediate comment on the federal investigation, and he must navigate simultaneous DOJ and House Ethics Committee inquiries while competing in the August primary against GOP rivals.

The DOJ's focus on overseas business dealings suggests federal prosecutors are examining conduct beyond the House Ethics Committee's scope, positioning Mills to face voter scrutiny in August as simultaneous investigations continue.
title: DOJ investigating Rep. Cory Mills: Sources
